original_abstract:
Finding a maximum independent set is a fundamental NP-hard problem with extensive real-world applications, requiring the identification of the largest subset of non-adjacent vertices in an undirected graph. In recent years, branch-and-bound and branch-and-reduce algorithms have emerged as the most effective exact solution methods. The branch-and-reduce framework, in particular, has successfully tackled previously intractable instances by integrating sophisticated reduction rules. However, while improvements in reduction techniques have driven recent progress, other critical algorithmic components—such as the branching strategy used to select the next vertex—have remained relatively under-explored. For years, the standard heuristic has simply been to branch on the vertex with the highest degree.

In this work, we introduce a Graph Neural Network (GNN) approach designed to optimize branching decisions. Given that the internal complexity of modern solvers makes traditional supervised or reinforcement learning difficult to implement, we employ a population-based genetic algorithm to evolve the model’s parameters. Our results demonstrate that this learned branching strategy outperforms traditional heuristics, achieving a speedup on 73% of benchmark instances with a median performance gain of 24%.
humanized_abstract:
Finding a maximum independent set is one of the most important NP-hard graph problems and has many practical applications. In particular, it consists of maximizing the size of an independent set of a graph, that is, maximizing the size of a set of non-adjacent vertices in an undirected graph. Exact methods of choice are nowadays based on a branch-and-bound framework and more recently on a branch-and-reduce framework which combines very efficiently, as we demonstrated, a set of strong reduction rules. Within the branch-and-reduce framework, further advances on solutions that were until now thought to be infeasible to solve exactly have been obtained in practice by developing more reductions. Consequently, other components of the algorithms have not been thoroughly investigated, in particular the way the graph is split in its next branch, an essential choice that has been performed since years by choosing the vertex with the highest degree.

We present a novel Graph Neural Network (GNN) to decide how to branch in a solver. Due to the inherent complexity of many state-of-the-art implementations, supervised and reinforcement learning are not practical options. Therefore, we use a population-based genetic algorithm to evolve the parameters of our model. Our experiments show that, when used as a drop-in replacement of the commonly used rules such as most positive, most negative or most frequent value, the learned strategy of branching decisions leads to improved performance. For 73% of the instances of our benchmark set, the solver is faster and with a median speedup of 24%.

document.input:
Finding a maximum independent set is a fundamental NP-hard problem with extensive real-world applications, requiring the identification of the largest subset of non-adjacent vertices in an undirected graph. In recent years, branch-and-bound and branch-and-reduce algorithms have emerged as the most effective exact solution methods. The branch-and-reduce framework, in particular, has successfully tackled previously intractable instances by integrating sophisticated reduction rules. However, while improvements in reduction techniques have driven recent progress, other critical algorithmic components—such as the branching strategy used to select the next vertex—have remained relatively under-explored.
